did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning models ResNet50 (CNN) and Vision Transformer (ViT) for multi-class skin lesion classification on the HAM10000 dataset. Features were extracted using these AI models and classified using an Artificial Neural Network (ANN), with performance evaluated via accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work comparing ResNet50 and Vision Transformer models for skin lesion classification on the HAM10000 dataset. It details specific methodology including feature extraction, PCA, RFE, and ANN classification, and reports original results such as accuracy metrics (e.g., 88.72%, 88.92%, 89.32%). Although published in a conference proceedings (ICCSC), it contains original research findings rather than being a review or survey.

title: Enhancing Skin Lesion Classification with ResNet and ViT: A Study on Feature Combination and Selection
journal: 2025 International Conference on Circuit, Systems and Communication (ICCSC)
brief_summary: This study compares ResNet50 and Vision Transformer (ViT) architectures for multi-class skin lesion classification using the HAM10000 dataset. Features extracted from both models were combined and processed using Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE) to enhance performance. The models were classified using an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) with hyperparameters optimized via Adam optimizer and categorical cross-entropy loss. ResNet50 achieved an accuracy of 88.72%, while ViT reached 88.92%, demonstrating comparable effectiveness. Feature fusion maintained similar accuracy, and PCA/RFE reduced dimensionality to 50 features without significant performance loss, achieving up to 89.32% accuracy. The results highlight that dimensionality reduction can improve efficiency while preserving essential classification information.

lesion_types_studied.details: Mentioned in the abstract and introduction as a key malignant condition for early detection. The study performs multi-class classification on the HAM10000 dataset which includes melanoma, but specific counts or per-class metrics for melanoma are not explicitly reported in the provided text.

The study uses the HAM10000 dataset which contains 7 classes of common pigmented skin lesions. The paper mentions 'multi-class skin lesion classification' and references the dataset's metadata file 'HAM10000_metadata.csv' which contains lesion types, but does not list the specific 7 class names (e.g., BCC, AK, BKL, DF, MEL, NV, VASC) in the provided text.

all_task_types.target: Multi-class skin lesion classes (7 classes from HAM10000 dataset)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class skin lesion classification using the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (lesion types) using an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) classifier fed by features extracted from ResNet50 and Vision Transformer (ViT). Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, sourced from multiple sources. The study utilized the metadata file 'HAM10000_metadata.csv' to load the dataset and retrieve lesion types and labels.
classes_used: 7 classes of common pigmented skin lesions

use.training: Used to extract features via ResNet50 and ViT, and to train the Artificial Neural Network (ANN) with a 20% validation split for monitoring performance.
use.validation: A 20% split of the dataset was used as a validation set during ANN training to monitor performance and mitigate overfitting.
use.testing: Used to evaluate final classification accuracy and compute metrics such as precision, recall, and F1-score.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Dimensionality reduction of combined feature sets to preserve 95% of variance and mitigate overfitting.
Feature selection to identify the 50 most significant features from the combined feature set using a Support Vector Machine (SVM) with an RBF kernel (C=1).
To evaluate classification performance and insights into how well models differentiated between various skin lesion classes.
To provide a comprehensive evaluation of model effectiveness, balancing false positives and false negatives across different skin lesion classes.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between ResNet50, ViT, and feature selection methods were based on accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics without statistical significance testing.

architecture_used.main_model: ResNet50 (CNN) and Vision Transformer (ViT) for feature extraction; Artificial Neural Network (ANN) for classification
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained versions of ResNet50 and ViT were used for feature extraction

Feature Fusion.description: Combined feature vectors from ResNet50 and ViT

ANN.activation: Activation functions optimized for binary classification (note: context mentions binary optimization but task is multi-class)

classification_or_output_head.input_features: Extracted features from ResNet50, ViT, or their combination (with/without PCA/RFE)

preprocessing_techniques: Image Resizing: All images were resized to 224x224 pixels to match the input requirements of ResNet50 and ViT.
Pixel Normalization: Pixel intensity values were normalized by dividing by 255 to ensure values fall within the range [0,1], stabilizing training and improving convergence.
Data Type Conversion: Images were converted into NumPy arrays after resizing and normalization for efficient storage and computation.
conclusions: ResNet50 and Vision Transformer (ViT) demonstrated comparable effectiveness for multi-class skin lesion classification on the HAM10000 dataset, achieving accuracies of 88.72% and 88.92%, respectively.

Feature fusion combining ResNet50 and ViT features maintained an accuracy of 88.72%, indicating that the combination did not provide significant performance improvements over the individual models in this specific study.

Dimensionality reduction techniques, specifically Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE), successfully reduced the feature space to 50 features without significant loss in accuracy.
PCA reduced features to 50 dimensions while preserving 95% of variance, achieving a classification accuracy of 89.32%.
RFE identified the 50 most significant features from the combined feature set, achieving an accuracy of 89.27%, demonstrating that many extracted features were redundant.
The study suggests that ViT may perform better with further fine-tuning or on larger datasets, as it currently shows comparable but not superior performance to ResNet50.

The combination of CNN and ViT architectures, along with feature selection, offers a viable approach for enhancing computational efficiency and maintaining robust skin lesion classification performance.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have class imbalance and demographic biases; no external validation on independent datasets was performed.

The paper does not report per-class metrics (precision, recall, F1-score) for individual lesion types, only aggregate macro/micro averages, limiting assessment of performance on rare or critical classes like melanoma.

ResNet50 reported 0.00% precision, recall, and F1-score in the provided results table, which is anomalous for a model with 88.72% accuracy and suggests a potential reporting error or specific metric calculation issue (e.g., micro vs. macro averaging confusion) that requires clarification.

Feature fusion (ResNet50 + ViT) yielded lower accuracy (88.72%) than ViT alone (88.92%) and PCA-reduced features (89.32%), indicating that simple concatenation did not improve performance and may have introduced noise or redundancy.

The ANN classifier activation functions were described as 'optimized for binary classification' despite the task being multi-class (7 classes), which is a methodological inconsistency or unclear description.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the small performance differences between models (e.g., 88.72% vs 88.92%).

The study uses pre-trained models without fine-tuning the feature extractors, which limits the ability to assess the full potential of ViT or the specific suitability of the pre-trained weights for dermatoscopic images.

Data leakage risks are not explicitly addressed; it is unclear if preprocessing steps (like normalization statistics) were computed on the entire dataset before splitting or only on the training fold.

The paper is a conference proceeding with limited depth in clinical relevance discussion; it does not address deployment constraints, computational cost comparisons, or real-world clinical workflow integration.
